narrative_blocks.body: Edward's middle name 'Kirtland' came from his maternal grandmother Lucilla Kirtland Gridley — the same surname his uncle John Kirtland Gridley had inherited as a middle name a generation earlier. The Kirtland surname passed through three Gridley-Fowler generations as a middle name without anyone in the family carrying it as a last name.

narrative_blocks.header: Drowned in Lake Michigan at forty-one
narrative_blocks.body: Harriet E. Olger Walker drowned at Grand Haven on Lake Michigan on August 15, 1926, at age forty-one. Her sister Hazel had married Harriet's husband's brother fifteen years earlier — two Olger sisters had married two Walker brothers, and both sisters died young.

naming_inspiration.description: Carries his paternal grandfather Edward Kirtland Fowler's first name.

narrative_blocks.header: Initials O.S. obscured his full given name
narrative_blocks.body: Source genealogies record his given name only as 'O. S. Edward Fowler' — the words behind the initials O. and S. were never written down.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at sixty-four, remarried in Florida
narrative_blocks.body: After his wife Dora died in 1959 he wintered in Florida like many in his family. In Largo, Pinellas County, on February 3, 1960, he married Winifred Brooks Vroman — a Michigan-born widow herself who carried the same maiden surname as his own mother Nellie Pearl Brooks. He lived another 33 years after Dora's death, reaching ninety-eight.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Eunice' likely honors her paternal grandmother Eunice Tower Eddy Fowler (X02661, 1841-1928), who was alive at her birth in 1900. Could alternatively honor her aunt Lula Eunice Fowler Richmond (HD3512, b. 1878).

narrative_blocks.header: Lived 102 years spanning the twentieth century
narrative_blocks.body: Ketha Eunice Fowler Parsons was born in St. Louis Michigan in August 1900 and died in Dunedin Florida in November 2002 at age 102. Her life spanned the entire twentieth century — from McKinley to George W. Bush, from the horse-and-buggy to the smartphone era.

narrative_blocks.header: Anchored the family's Florida snowbird path
narrative_blocks.body: Ketha and her husband settled in Dunedin on Florida's Gulf coast. Her mother Nellie Pearl Brooks Fowler also died in Dunedin in 1960, and her older brother O. S.

narrative_blocks.header: Youngest of four long-lived Richmond sisters
narrative_blocks.body: Edith was the youngest of four daughters of Earl Richmond and Lula Eunice Fowler Richmond. None of the four died young: Luella reached 77, Lois 71, Lillian 85, and Edith herself 87 — the kind of generational survival pattern unusual in Michigan working-class families of their era. All four stayed in Gratiot or Ingham counties their whole lives.

narrative_blocks.header: Her diploma came in 1971, family grown
narrative_blocks.body: With her six children raised, Edith set out for the goal she had postponed and earned her St. Louis High School diploma in 1971. She was then in her late fifties.

burial.plot_notes: Churches Corners Cemetery, Wheatland Township Hillsdale County MI.

parents.father_research_notes: Abel Fowler of Churchville NY. Per EH 1909 entry 2242 and the published Fowler genealogy 'Descendants of Abel Fowler.'
parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Eddy Fowler. Per EH 1909 entry 2242.

narrative_blocks.header: Origin of the name Anson remains uncertain
narrative_blocks.body: Anson was born in Fairfield Herkimer County NY in 1809 to Abel and Mary Eddy Fowler — but the Fowler line shows no documented earlier Anson. Genealogists have suggested the name may trace to Mary's possible Eddy or Ross matrilineal kin: a William Anson Eddy and an Anson Ross were both born around 1809, and three Ross sisters had married three Eddy brothers in that family network. Whether the name came from those collateral relatives or from a popular contemporary figure named Anson is unresolved.

narrative_blocks.header: Frontier child became Michigan land-patent pioneer
narrative_blocks.body: Anson was about seven when his parents moved the family west from Herkimer County NY to Genesee County NY around 1816. After marrying Harriet Louisa Gridley in Ogden NY in 1831, the couple bought land in Wheatland Township Hillsdale County Michigan from a federal land office in 1835 — second-wave New England migration into the Old Northwest. Three of their five surviving children were born in Wheatland on that patent.

sources.label: Melissa Craven Fowler — Fowler family genealogist

burial.plot_notes: Churches Corners Cemetery, Wheatland Township Hillsdale County MI. Per Abel Fowler genealogy.

narrative_blocks.body: Harriet Louisa Gridley and Anson Fowler married at Ogden NY in 1831 and later migrated to Wheatland Township in Hillsdale County Michigan — among the first Hooker-descended families to settle west of New York. Four of their five children were born in Michigan; Anson is buried at Churches Corners Cemetery there.

narrative_blocks.header: Charter member of frontier Free-Will Baptist Church
narrative_blocks.body: Harriet was an early member of the First Free-Will Baptist Church of Wheatland Michigan, organized February 19, 1838 in Eli Eastman's home shortly after the Fowlers arrived. The church gathered at Church's Corners just a few miles from her farm; local accounts noted that before the congregation organized, 'there was more rifle-shooting done on Sunday than any other day.'

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at forty-five, managed her own land deals
narrative_blocks.body: When Anson died in 1855 Harriet was forty-five, with five surviving children. Hillsdale County records show her buying and selling acreage within a few miles of the original 1835 patent for the next two decades — alone or jointly with relatives. She remained financially comfortable through the 1870 and 1880 censuses, outlived Anson by forty-three years, and died in Wheatland Township in 1898.

sources.label: Descendants of Abel Fowler (Wix-hosted PDF)
Melissa Craven Fowler — Fowler family genealogist

career.location: Kensington CT, Durham NY, Pompey NY, North Chili NY
career.notes: Farmed across multiple Connecticut and New York locations through his lifetime.

military_service.notes: Soldier of the War of 1812, stationed at Oswego NY — Lake Ontario port and frontline against British attack 1814.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name honors his great-great-great-grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for his ancestor Reverend Thomas Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker Gridley was given the full name of his great-great-great-grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford. The naming honored a preacher five generations removed who had died in 1647 — a 1775 echo of the Puritan founding.

narrative_blocks.header: War of 1812 soldier at Oswego frontier
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker Gridley served as a soldier at Oswego NY during the War of 1812. The Lake Ontario port was captured by the British in May 1814 and the fort burned. His specific role and fate during the engagement are not documented in EH 1909.

parents.father_research_notes: Daniel Kirtland of Durham NY (originally Saybrook CT). Per EH 1909 entry 1093.
parents.mother_research_notes: Lovisa Lord Kirtland of Durham NY. Per EH 1909 entry 1093.

burialCemetery.name: Oak Grove Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Oak Grove Cemetery, St. Louis, Gratiot County, Michigan. Burial place of Edward Kirtland Fowler (H03720, 1832-1898) and his wife Eunice Tower Eddy Fowler (X02661, 1841-1928). Distinct from Oak Grove cemeteries elsewhere. Per Descendants of Abel Fowler PDF.
